title: Resolving Incorrect Type Inference for Type Guards in a Generic Function
subCategory: TypeScript Generic Functions and Type Guards

statement.markdown:
Given a generic function `filterArray<T>()` that uses type guards to filter an array of objects based on a condition, the function has an issue with type inference. The function should return an array of objects that satisfy the condition, but due to the type guard, TypeScript incorrectly infers the type of the returned array. Fix the bug to ensure correct type inference for the returned array.

The function should take an array of objects and a condition function as input, and return an array of objects that satisfy the condition.
statement.inputFormat: An array of objects and a condition function
statement.outputFormat: An array of objects that satisfy the condition
statement.notes: Test the function with various input arrays and condition functions to ensure correct type inference.

constraints.description: A function that takes an object and returns a boolean value

examples.input: filterArray([{ id: 1, name: 'John' }, { id: 2, name: 'Jane' }], (obj) => obj.id % 2 === 0)
examples.explanation: The function should return an array with the object that has an even ID

examples.input: filterArray([{ id: 1, name: 'John' }, { id: 2, name: 'Jane' }, { id: 3, name: 'Bob' }], (obj) => obj.id > 1)
examples.explanation: The function should return an array with the objects that have an ID greater than 1

hints.text: Use the `as` keyword to assert the type of the returned array

editorial.title: Editorial: Resolving Incorrect Type Inference for Type Guards
editorial.intuition: The issue is caused by the type guard, which prevents TypeScript from inferring the correct type of the returned array.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to fix the issue is to use the `as` keyword to assert the type of the returned array, ensuring that TypeScript infers the correct type.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `as` keyword to assert the type of the returned array, ensuring that TypeScript infers the correct type.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is that the function returns an array of objects with the correct type, as required.
commonMistakes: Mistaking the type guard for the type of the returned array

functionSignature.javascript: function filterArray<T>(arr: T[], condition: (obj: T) => boolean): T[]

testCases.input: filterArray([{ id: 1, name: 'John' }, { id: 2, name: 'Jane' }], (obj) => obj.id % 2 === 0)
filterArray([{ id: 1, name: 'John' }, { id: 2, name: 'Jane' }, { id: 3, name: 'Bob' }], (obj) => obj.id > 1)
